nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes in clear, often deeper water on the northern Great Barrier Reef and Coral Sea; typically encountered between roughly 20–55 m, with shallower reports from 1 m

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, and other meaty foods; has been reported to also take high-quality marine flake and pellet food. In the wild, has been documented foraging on zooplankton and small benthic invertebrates above the reef substrate.

avoidWith: other male fairy wrasses in the same tank

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Jokuyken15; 5209x3473. Sharp aquarium side profile of a vibrant male showing the diagnostic blue horizontal lines across a red-orange body and orange eye ring; whole fish in frame, well lit. Best overall.
Photographer Debra Baker (Uatio, New Caledonia); 1699x1313. In-situ male displaying blue lines and the elongated pink-red caudal fan; slight underwater blue cast but diagnostic features clear.
Photographer Debra Baker (Uatio, New Caledonia); 1332x941. In-situ whole-fish view showing blue-line pattern and pink caudal patch; slightly soft, kept as variety.

summary: The Lineatus Fairy Wrasse is a prized saltwater species from the Coral Sea and northern Great Barrier Reef. Males display brilliant blue horizontal lines across a vivid red-orange body. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, it is a sought-after addition to larger reef aquariums.
careNotes: The Lineatus Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus lineatus) is a deeper-water species native to the Western Central Pacific, ranging from New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands north to the Great Barrier Reef and Coral Sea. In the wild it has been documented from roughly 1 m down to about 55 m on outer reef slopes, and most aquarium specimens are collected from the deeper end of that range. A tightly fitted lid or eurobrace is essential, as this species, like most fairy wrasses, has been known to jump. It is best kept as a single male or as one male paired with one or more females introduced simultaneously to establish a harem; adding females after the male is already settled may provoke territorial behavior. A sub-dominant male will also transition to dominant male coloration if kept as the only male in the tank. Like all Cirrhilabrus, the Lineatus Fairy Wrasse is a protogynous hermaphrodite (meaning individuals are born female and the dominant individual can transition to male), and it has been reported to form harems in larger systems. Distinct pairing during spawning has been documented in the wild. Due to its limited collection range, the species has historically commanded a premium price compared to more readily available fairy wrasses.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not commonly reported for this species. Like most fairy wrasses, spawning in the wild follows a haremic structure with a dominant male. Replicating that social arrangement in a large, well-established reef system may encourage nuptial display behavior, though successful reproduction in home aquariums has not been well documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page confirmed: care level easy, temperament peaceful, reef compatible yes, minimum tank 90 gal, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, diet carnivore (mysis/brine/meaty foods + marine flake/pellet), adult size 5 in, native locale Great Barrier Reef and the Coral Sea, family Labridae. Confirmed jumping behavior and recommendation for a tight-fitting canopy; sub-dominant male transitions to dominant coloration when kept alone.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max size 12.0 cm TL, native range Western Central Pacific (New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands to the Great Barrier Reef), depth 1–55 m on outer reef slopes in clear water, temperature 25.4–27.9°C (mean 26.5°C), family Labridae, order Eupercaria, distinct pairing during breeding. IUCN: Least Concern (2009). Temperature spread vs LiveAquaria's 22–26°C averaged to 24–27°C; size 12.0 cm TL agrees with LiveAquaria's 5 in (~12.7 cm).
